facts.text: South Beach’s Lummus Park Beach is a favorite for sunbathing and people‑watching, drawing crowds along the Atlantic shoreline.
The area’s historic Art Deco architecture invites beachgoers to lounge on the sand while admiring the colorful facades that define South Beach’s skyline.
South Beach was officially incorporated as a city on March 26, 1915, after John Collins dredged the Collins Canal to bring mangos to market.
During World War II, the Army Air Forces Technical Training Command trained a quarter of all enlisted men on South Beach, calling it the “most beautiful bootcamp in America.”
South Beach’s beachside promenade once hosted a bronze replica of a bust of Capitman, created by her mother, a sculptor and artist.
The neighborhood’s name honors Carl G., an automotive parts pioneer who helped shape the area’s early development.
